ontology.foodex2_description: Moussaka — layered baked dish of minced meat, eggplant, and béchamel sauce
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for moussaka as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT does not routinely code composite ethnic dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of lamb moussaka weighs approximately 450 g, providing around 750 kcal, 45 g protein, 50 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Moussaka, with meat (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for Turkish lamb moussaka exists. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy composite data for moussaka-type dishes (ground lamb, eggplant, potato, tomato sauce, béchamel) and cross-referenced against the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input per_100g values are within plausible range for this dish type and have been retained with minor rounding normalization. Potassium estimated at 330 mg/100g from USDA component data for eggplant, potato, and tomato. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available at composite level; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish lamb moussaka. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous baked meat-and-vegetable casserole entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (2008). The dish is dominated by protein (10 g/100g) and fat (11.1 g/100g) which substantially attenuate glycaemic response; carbohydrate (7.8 g/100g) derives primarily from potato and béchamel flour, partially offset by 1.8 g/100g dietary fibre. GL per serving = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 45/100 × 35.1 g = 15.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 200 mg/100g; a 450 g serving delivers 900 mg sodium, representing 39% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced added salt in the spice mix and béchamel; the dish's potassium content (~330 mg/100g from eggplant, potato, and tomato) provides partial offset but does not fully compensate for the sodium load at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.8 g/100g, GI ~45 low) with meaningful protein (10 g/100g) and fat (11.1 g/100g) that attenuate postprandial glycaemic response; however, a 450 g serving delivers ~35 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a controlled portion of 200–250 g (approximately 16–20 g carbohydrate per serving), pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side; the high saturated fat content (4.4 g/100g from lamb and butter) should be monitored as chronic intake may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.4 g/100g and cholesterol at 33.3 mg/100g from lamb mince and béchamel; a 450 g serving delivers ~20 g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit for CVD patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings of 150–200 g, request preparation with olive oil instead of butter, and choose leaner cuts or substitute lamb with turkey mince to reduce saturated fat and LDL-raising dietary cholesterol.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; eggplant, potato, and tomato are nightshade vegetables that may trigger solanine-mediated joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, substitute lamb with chicken or turkey, replace butter with olive oil, and monitor for joint flare following nightshade vegetable intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb moussaka (also spelled mousakka) is a classic layered baked casserole originating from the Eastern Mediterranean and widely associated with Turkish and Greek cuisines. The dish is constructed from alternating layers of thinly sliced eggplant (aubergine) and potato, seasoned minced lamb cooked with tomato sauce, onion, and warm spices such as cinnamon and allspice, all topped with a thick, oven-browned béchamel sauce made from butter, flour, milk, and egg. The assembled dish is baked until golden and set, yielding a rich, hearty texture with savoury-sweet spice notes from the lamb and a creamy, slightly nutty topping. Per 100 g, lamb moussaka provides approximately 167 kcal, 10 g protein, 11.1 g fat (4.4 g saturated), 7.8 g carbohydrate, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 200 mg sodium. A standard restaurant serving of 450 g delivers roughly 750 kcal and 35 g carbohydrate. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), as the high protein and fat content substantially attenuate postprandial glucose response. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium load at full serving, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to carbohydrate from potato and béchamel flour, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from lamb and butter. It is not recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid content in red meat and nightshade vegetables. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified lamb. It contains common allergens: milk, egg, and wheat (gluten from béchamel flour).
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb moussaka?
Is moussaka good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry for Turkish lamb moussaka exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy composite data for moussaka-type dishes and are consistent with the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for composite lamb moussaka not available in USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite baked dish not reported in the adapted USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient panel for composite lamb moussaka not fully reported in the adapted USDA source as of 2024. Potassium estimated at 330 mg/100g from USDA component-level data for eggplant, potato, and tomato. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is sold via GrabFood Singapore under the name 'Mousakka Lamb'; the canonical English name has been normalized to 'Moussaka with Lamb'. Ethnic tags 'Kebab' and 'Shawarma' from the input record are not applicable to moussaka and have been excluded. GL per serving calculated as: GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 45/100 × 35.1 = 15.8.
